rationales.A: Outpatient management without workup is unsafe at this age; the under-3-months febrile-infant rule overrides reassuring appearance. Would be acceptable only if the child were over 3 months, well, and meeting NICE green-zone criteria (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child).
rationales.C: Omitting LP is the classic anti-pattern in under-8-week infants: low-risk biomarkers do not exclude meningitis. Selective workup without LP applies only to well 8-week to 3-month infants meeting all Rochester/Philadelphia low-risk criteria (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child).
rationales.D: PO amoxicillin gives inadequate CSF penetration and serum levels for neonatal sepsis or meningitis; oral therapy is never empirical cover in suspected SBI under 3 months. Would be reasonable only for a confirmed mild outpatient bacterial focus (e.g. acute otitis media) in an older well child (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
rationales.E: CXR is a focus-driven investigation, not a sepsis screen, and substitutes for none of the required cultures. Would be indicated only with respiratory signs (tachypnoea out of proportion, focal crackles, hypoxia) (Source: NICE NG143).

rationales.A: Adrenaline before adequate volume worsens outcomes by increasing afterload on an under-filled ventricle. Would be correct only after 40-60 mL/kg crystalloid in fluid-refractory shock, and even then peripheral adrenaline 0.05-0.1 microg/kg/min is started while central access is obtained (Source: ANZICS Paediatric Sepsis Pathway).
rationales.B: Three errors stacked: oral antibiotic in shock, sub-therapeutic 10 mL/kg bolus, and imaging-before-antibiotics. CT head is not required prior to antibiotics even with suspected meningitis; antibiotics are given first and CT/LP follow once stable (Source: ANZICS; RCH CPG Meningitis).
rationales.D: Treating meningococcal shock as a viral fever is the cardinal sepsis miss. Antipyretic-and-observe would only be appropriate for a well-appearing child with a clear viral focus and no red flags (Source: ANZICS Paediatric Sepsis Pathway).
rationales.E: LP is contraindicated in shock or raised ICP and must never delay antibiotics. LP-first would be acceptable only in a stable well-perfused child with isolated meningism, normal GCS and no coagulopathy (Source: RCH CPG: Meningitis).

rationales.A: Multiple Gorelick signs and a measured weight deficit are present, so dehydration is clinically detectable. Under 3 percent would require fewer than 2 Gorelick signs, no measured weight loss, and intact tears, mucosae, and skin turgor (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis).
rationales.C: Severe (10 percent plus) dehydration requires obtundation, prolonged central CR over 3 seconds, weak pulses and absent tears; over-classifying triggers unnecessary IV resuscitation. Bolus saline would be correct only with shock features or true severe deficit (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis).
rationales.D: Intubation for hypovolaemic shock is reserved for failed resuscitation with persistent altered consciousness or airway compromise; this child has preserved BP, GCS and central perfusion. Decompensated shock would require absent peripheral pulses and altered mental state (Source: APLS).
rationales.E: Measured weight loss and clinical signs prove a deficit; calling this euvolaemic and discharging is the classic anti-pattern of trusting appearance over weight. Reassure-only is acceptable only when serial weights and clinical exam are both normal (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis).

rationales.A: IV is reserved for shock, severe dehydration, ileus, altered mental state or failed enteral therapy; jumping to IV in mild-moderate dehydration is the AU anti-pattern when NG ORS is well validated. Would be correct with shock or failed NG trial (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis).
rationales.B: 0.45 percent saline is no longer recommended as routine paediatric IV maintenance in Australia due to hospital-acquired hyponatraemia. Isotonic fluid (Plasma-Lyte or 0.9 percent saline with 5 percent dextrose) is standard if IV is required (Source: NHMRC Paediatric IV Fluids; RCH CPG IV Fluids).
rationales.C: Plain water and dilute juice lack adequate sodium and glucose, risking hyponatraemia in ongoing losses; this is the home-remedy anti-pattern. ORS or commercial hydration solutions are required, and ED rehydration is needed given failed oral intake (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis).
rationales.E: Ondansetron enables ORS but does not replace it; single-dose-and-discharge without verified intake misses ongoing deficit. Would be appropriate only as an adjunct before a documented successful oral trial (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis).

rationales.B: Treating anaphylactic wheeze as asthma is the killer anti-pattern; every minute of adrenaline delay increases mortality. Salbutamol-first is acceptable only in isolated bronchospasm without other anaphylaxis features (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines).
rationales.C: Antihistamines and steroids do not treat airway oedema, bronchospasm or hypotension and have no role as first-line. They are adjuncts after adrenaline, never the initial intervention (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines).
rationales.D: 300 microgram is the EpiPen dose for over 20 kg under ASCIA; at exactly 20 kg the 150 microgram EpiPen Jr is correct. Would be correct if the child were over 20 kg (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Action Plan).
rationales.E: Talking and responsive does not exclude anaphylaxis; hoarse voice and throat tightness are airway red flags requiring immediate adrenaline. Withholding while awaiting paramedics is the textbook delay-to-death error (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines).

rationales.A: 1 mg IV (1:10,000) is the cardiac arrest dose and will cause hypertensive crisis, myocardial ischaemia or VF in a perfusing patient. Would be correct only in cardiac arrest, not in refractory anaphylaxis with a pulse (Source: ASCIA; ARC Paediatric ALS).
rationales.B: Steroids/antihistamines do nothing for refractory shock and promethazine is contraindicated under 2 years (paradoxical reactions, hypotension). Only ever adjuncts after definitive adrenaline therapy (Source: ASCIA; TGA Promethazine warning).
rationales.D: After two failed IM doses, peripheral perfusion is poor and IM absorption unreliable; persisting with IM is the refractory-anaphylaxis pitfall. IV infusion is the next step (Source: ASCIA Refractory Anaphylaxis Guidelines).
rationales.E: Glucagon has a narrow niche: beta-blocked patients with refractory shock unresponsive to adrenaline. It is an adjunct, not a replacement for adrenaline infusion (Source: ASCIA Refractory Anaphylaxis Guidelines).

rationales.A: Re-dosing buccal once IV is in place wastes a more reliable route and risks under-dosing through ongoing buccal pooling. Buccal would be the right second dose only if IV access were still unavailable (Source: RCH CPG: Status Epilepticus).
rationales.B: Phenytoin/levetiracetam is the second-line step after two adequate benzodiazepine doses; only one has been given. Would be correct if two benzodiazepine doses had already failed (Source: RCH CPG: Status Epilepticus).
rationales.C: Phenobarbital is reserved for refractory status after benzodiazepines plus a second-line agent have failed; jumping straight to it skips the standard ladder. Would be the right step in established refractory status (Source: APLS Australia).
rationales.D: RSI with thiopentone is the refractory-status escalation only after benzodiazepines and at least one second-line agent fail, with airway/breathing currently maintained the patient should not be intubated yet. Would be correct in refractory status with airway compromise (Source: APLS Australia).

rationales.B: Starting an antiepileptic after a single unprovoked seizure is the textbook over-treatment error; recurrence is around 40 percent and early AED does not improve long-term outcome. Would be considered only after a second seizure or with high-risk EEG/MRI findings (Source: RCH CPG: First Seizure; NICE NG217).
rationales.C: CT plus LP for a brief generalised seizure with full recovery is the over-investigation anti-pattern: no fever, no meningism, no focal deficit, no trauma. Indicated only with persistent altered GCS, focal deficit, suspected meningitis, or trauma (Source: RCH CPG: First Seizure).
rationales.D: A rescue kit is not standard after a single brief seizure; first-fit pathway is EEG-led follow-up, not pre-emptive home benzodiazepines. Would be appropriate only for established epilepsy or known prolonged seizures over 5 minutes (Source: RCH CPG: First Seizure).
rationales.E: Same-day MRI is over-imaging for a generalised seizure with normal exam; outpatient MRI is the standard timing. Urgent MRI is reserved for focal seizures, persistent deficit, age under 2 years, or refractory seizures (Source: RCH CPG: First Seizure).

rationales.A: EEG and MRI are not indicated after a simple febrile seizure with full recovery and normal exam; this is the febrile-seizure over-investigation anti-pattern. Would be appropriate only for complex febrile seizures (focal, prolonged over 15 minutes, or recurrent within 24 hours) (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Seizure).
rationales.B: Routine LP for age under 2 is the legacy rule that AU guidelines have abandoned; a well, fully immunised child without meningism or persistent altered conscious state does not need CSF. Would be indicated only with meningism, persistent drowsiness, or incomplete Hib/pneumococcal vaccination (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Seizure).
rationales.C: Scheduled antipyretics do not reduce recurrence and may delay recognition of further illness; use for comfort only. Would never be correct as a prophylactic strategy (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Seizure).
rationales.E: Maintenance AEDs are not used for simple febrile seizures regardless of recurrence rate; risks outweigh benefit. Considered only for very prolonged or frequent complex febrile seizures under specialist review (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Seizure).

rationales.A: No cardiac history, gallop, hepatomegaly or pulmonary crackles to suggest pump failure; mislabelling sepsis as cardiogenic delays fluid resuscitation. Would be correct with known cardiomyopathy, post-cardiac-surgery, or signs of failure (Source: APLS Australia).
rationales.B: BP is preserved so by definition this is compensated shock; jumping to adrenaline before fluid breaks the resuscitation order. Vasoactives are added only once 40-60 mL/kg of crystalloid has failed (Source: APLS Australia; Surviving Sepsis Campaign Paediatric 2020).
rationales.C: Cap refill 4 s, mottling, oliguria and lactate 3.6 is not normal; calling this 'no shock' is the well-looking-but-decompensating trap. Maintenance-only would be correct with normal perfusion, vitals and lactate (Source: APLS Australia).
rationales.D: Warm shock shows flash cap refill, bounding pulses and wide pulse pressure; this child has the opposite (cold shock). Noradrenaline-first would be appropriate in fluid-refractory warm septic shock, not cold (Source: APLS Australia; Surviving Sepsis Paediatric 2020).

rationales.B: Hepatomegaly and crackles signal volume overload; further large boluses worsen pulmonary oedema and hypoxaemia. This is the fluid-overload paediatric-sepsis trap; reassess after 40-60 mL/kg and switch to inotrope (Source: Surviving Sepsis Paediatric 2020).
rationales.C: Noradrenaline is the warm-shock first-line; cold shock with poor cardiac output is treated with adrenaline. Would be correct in fluid-refractory shock with wide pulse pressure and warm peripheries (Source: APLS Australia).
rationales.D: Dopamine showed higher mortality than adrenaline in the Ventura 2015 paediatric RCT and is no longer first-line. Considered only when adrenaline/noradrenaline unavailable (Source: Surviving Sepsis Paediatric 2020).
rationales.E: Hydrocortisone is an adjunct for catecholamine-resistant shock or proven/suspected adrenal insufficiency, not a substitute for inotropes. Would be added on top of adrenaline if shock persists despite escalating doses (Source: Surviving Sepsis Paediatric 2020).

rationales.A: Paediatric drowning with ROSC and short submersion has documented good neuro outcomes; early withdrawal is the prognostic-nihilism error. Withdrawal discussion is appropriate only after 48-72 h of intensive care and clear secondary-survey/imaging confirmation of devastating injury (Source: ANZCOR Drowning).
rationales.B: Hyperoxia and hypocapnia cause cerebral vasoconstriction and oxidative reperfusion injury post-arrest; target normoxia and normocapnia. Hyperventilation is acceptable only for transient management of impending herniation (Source: ANZCOR Post-Resuscitation Care).
rationales.C: Induced hyperthermia worsens neurological outcomes; controlled rewarming to 36 degC is the standard. Empirical antibiotics are reserved for documented aspiration of contaminated water or clinical evidence of infection (Source: ANZCOR; RCH CPG Drowning).
rationales.D: Drowning-related pulmonary oedema and ARDS can be delayed up to 8 hours; clear CXR does not justify early extubation when GCS is low and B-lines are present. Extubation is appropriate only with normal gas exchange, GCS over 12 and resolved B-lines (Source: ANZCOR).

rationales.A: Colloid worsens lung water in the early capillary-leak phase of burn injury; crystalloid is mandated in the first 24 h. Albumin is added only in the second 24 h or as rescue in failed resuscitation (Source: ANZBA EMSB).
rationales.B: Two errors: omitting maintenance (children always need maintenance plus burn fluid) and using 0.9 percent saline (hyperchloraemic acidosis risk). Parkland-only would be acceptable in adults; Hartmann's plus separate maintenance is the paediatric standard (Source: RCH CPG: Burns; ANZBA).
rationales.C: Hypertonic saline is not standard in paediatric burn resuscitation in Australia and is studied only in specific adult settings. Would never be first-line in children (Source: ANZBA EMSB).
rationales.E: TBSA over 10 percent in children mandates formal burn resuscitation fluid in addition to maintenance; an 18 percent burn cannot be managed with maintenance alone. Maintenance-only is appropriate only for under-10-percent TBSA (Source: RCH CPG: Burns).

rationales.A: Delaying NAC when the level is already above the nomogram treatment line costs hepatoprotective efficacy (best within 8 h of ingestion). Repeat-and-wait would be reasonable only for an asymptomatic patient with a sub-line level and uncertain timing (Source: Australian PIC Paracetamol Guideline).
rationales.C: Activated charcoal needs to be within 1-2 h (or up to 4 h for sustained-release); at 5 h post-ingestion it is not useful and must never delay NAC. Would be appropriate within 1-2 hours of ingestion (Source: Australian PIC).
rationales.D: 165 mg/L at 4.5 h is above the 150 mg/L treatment line, and 230 mg/kg exceeds the 200 mg/kg dose threshold; the higher of dose-or-level governs. Would be appropriate only if BOTH dose under 200 mg/kg AND level below line (Source: Australian PIC).
rationales.E: Oral methionine is not used in Australia and transplant assessment is for established fulminant failure meeting King's College criteria, not initial presentation. NAC is the standard antidote (Source: AMH; Australian PIC).

rationales.B: Subglottic haemangioma gives biphasic (not purely inspiratory) stridor and often a beard-distribution cutaneous haemangioma; classically worsens with crying not posture. Would be the answer with biphasic stridor and visible facial haemangioma (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor).
rationales.C: Croup is acute, with barking cough, hoarse cry, fever and age 6 months to 3 years; not chronic stridor from the neonatal period. Would be correct with acute onset and barking cough (Source: RCH CPG: Croup).
rationales.D: Inhaled foreign body presents acutely with a witnessed choking episode and focal asymmetry; chronic positional stridor since birth excludes it. Would be correct with sudden-onset cough and unilateral wheeze in a toddler (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Vascular rings give biphasic stridor with feeding difficulty and reflux, often worse with neck extension, not improvement with prone positioning. Would be correct with feeding difficulty and stridor worse extending the neck (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor).

rationales.A: RR 58 is expected in bronchiolitis and not itself an admission threshold; using it would admit nearly everyone. Tachypnoea matters only when combined with hypoxaemia, severe WOB or feeding compromise (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis).
rationales.C: Low-grade fever and household viral contact are typical bronchiolitis features in a stable infant over 6 weeks; sepsis workup is the over-investigation trap. Sepsis workup is required under 8 weeks regardless of focus (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; RCH CPG: Febrile Child).
rationales.D: HR 162 is age-appropriate with fever and WOB; treating it as primary tachyarrhythmia is the misread. Would warrant workup only if persistent at rest, with abnormal complexes on ECG (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis).
rationales.E: Salbutamol is not recommended in bronchiolitis under 12 months (no proven benefit, may worsen oxygenation). Wheeze in a toddler over 12 months with prior episodes might warrant a salbutamol trial (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis).

rationales.B: MDI-spacer is mild-moderate territory; severe asthma with SpO2 90 percent demands nebulised therapy and immediate oral steroid, not registrar-gated dosing. Spacer would suit a mild attack with SpO2 over 94 percent (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma).
rationales.C: IV magnesium is escalation after inhaled bronchodilator plus oral steroid have failed, not first-line; skipping inhaled therapy denies definitive bronchodilation. Would be correct as second-line in severe asthma not responding to first hour of inhaled therapy (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma).
rationales.D: Antibiotics are not routine in acute asthma without focal signs or documented bacterial focus; 4-hourly nebs is sub-therapeutic for severe disease. Continuous nebs are required initially in severe asthma (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma).
rationales.E: Nebulised adrenaline is for croup; oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg (not dexamethasone) is the standard asthma steroid in Australia. Would suit moderate croup with stridor at rest (Source: RCH CPG: Croup; RCH CPG: Asthma).

rationales.A: Normal coags do not exclude abuse, and a bleeding disorder cannot account for patterned bruising in a pre-mobile infant. Outpatient coags alone would be appropriate only if there were no concerning history/pattern and an isolated bruise with positive family bleeding history (Source: RCH CPG: Non-Accidental Injury).
rationales.B: Bruises in a pre-mobile infant are sentinel injuries: 'those who don't cruise rarely bruise.' Reassure-and-discharge is the textbook safeguarding failure. Reassurance is appropriate only after full workup is reassuring (Source: RCH CPG: Non-Accidental Injury).
rationales.D: Mandatory reporting is triggered by reasonable suspicion, not confirmed imaging; delaying notification breaches the statutory duty under state Children and Young Persons Acts. Would never be correct to defer notification pending results (Source: NSW/Vic mandatory reporter legislation; RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Direct confrontation by the treating team can compromise the child's safety, the forensic investigation and ongoing therapeutic relationships; that role belongs to child protection and police. Investigation and notification proceed without confrontation (Source: RCH CPG: Non-Accidental Injury).

rationales.A: Mouthpiece is preferred over 4 years if lip seal is reliable; switching back to a face-mask reduces drug delivery in older children. Face-mask is correct only under 4 years or when lip seal fails (Source: National Asthma Council).
rationales.B: Spacers are washed monthly with detergent and air-dried; towel-drying generates static that reduces drug delivery. This is the classic spacer-technique pitfall to teach families (Source: National Asthma Council).
rationales.C: 5-10 second breath-hold is enough; 30 seconds adds no benefit and most children cannot comply. Anti-pattern is over-prescribing impractical instructions (Source: National Asthma Council).
rationales.E: DPIs need a fast inspiratory flow that many 8-year-olds cannot generate reliably, especially when unwell. DPI would be appropriate only for a teen with confirmed adequate inspiratory flow (Source: National Asthma Council).

rationales.A: Low-flow O2 does not unload respiratory muscles or prevent apnoea; nasal prongs cap out at sub-therapeutic support for type 2 failure. Would be appropriate only with mild work of breathing and SpO2 needs alone (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis).
rationales.B: Hypertonic saline and salbutamol are not recommended in routine bronchiolitis in AU guidelines; they distract from definitive escalation. Would be considered only in older toddlers with viral-induced wheeze (Source: PREDICT Bronchiolitis Guideline).
rationales.D: Intubation is reserved for failure of non-invasive support (HFNC/CPAP), persistent apnoea, exhaustion or progressive hypercapnia despite optimisation. Premature intubation increases ventilator-associated complications. Would be correct after a failed HFNC trial (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis).
rationales.E: Steroids and antibiotics are not indicated in uncomplicated viral bronchiolitis and do nothing for impending respiratory failure. Antibiotics only with proven bacterial co-infection or sepsis (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Aminophylline is third-line after IV magnesium fails; narrow therapeutic index with arrhythmia and seizure risk. Would be correct in PICU after magnesium and IV salbutamol failure (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma).
rationales.B: Nebulised adrenaline is the croup drug; in asthma it offers no benefit over salbutamol and may cause hypertensive effects. Would be correct in severe croup with stridor at rest (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma; RCH CPG: Croup).
rationales.C: IV salbutamol is reserved for after IV magnesium has failed; using it first risks worsening the existing beta-agonist-driven lactic acidosis and tachyarrhythmia. Would be appropriate after magnesium trial with persistent severe asthma (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma).
rationales.D: Oral prednisolone is equipotent to IV hydrocortisone if absorbed; doubling up adds toxicity (hyperglycaemia, hypokalaemia) without benefit. IV hydrocortisone is correct only when oral is not tolerated (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma; National Asthma Handbook).

rationales.B: Azithromycin covers atypicals (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia); these are uncommon in 4-year-olds with lobar consolidation, where pneumococcus dominates. Would be correct in school-age children with subacute symptoms or extrapulmonary features (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: IV is for children failing oral or with severe disease (SpO2 under 92 percent, severe distress, complicated CAP with effusion). This child tolerates oral. Would be correct with hypoxia, vomiting, or pleural effusion (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines; RCH CPG: CAP).
rationales.D: Augmentin adds beta-lactamase cover not required for pneumococcal CAP; reserved for aspiration, complicated CAP or amoxicillin failure. Would be correct for aspiration pneumonia or amoxicillin failure (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Flucloxacillin targets Staph aureus (cavitations, pneumatocoeles, necrotising). Would be correct for staphylococcal pneumonia features (rapidly progressive, post-influenza, cavitating) (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines; RCH CPG: CAP).

rationales.A: Tachypnoea is a severity marker but non-specific; both viral and bacterial pneumonia produce it. Would never alone discriminate aetiology (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Childhood pneumonia).
rationales.B: Lobar consolidation supports bacterial cause but viral pneumonia (RSV, adenovirus, influenza) can produce lobar pattern, and bacterial can be patchy. Biomarkers are more specific. Would be more discriminating if combined with high procalcitonin (Source: RCH CPG: CAP).
rationales.C: CRP rises in both viral and bacterial disease; an isolated high CRP without neutrophilia or procalcitonin has poor specificity. Would be more discriminating combined with procalcitonin over 0.5 (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.E: Both bacterial and viral pneumonia commonly follow a viral URTI and sibling contact is non-discriminating. Would discriminate only with extrapulmonary atypical features (e.g. rash, haemolysis suggesting Mycoplasma) (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.A: Normal CXR does not exclude a radiolucent foreign body (most peanuts and plastics); a witnessed choking episode plus focal signs mandates bronchoscopy. Discharge is the missed-aspiration anti-pattern that leads to chronic lung damage (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign Body Aspiration).
rationales.B: Focal monophonic wheeze after a choking episode is not asthma; salbutamol trial delays diagnosis. Would be appropriate only with diffuse polyphonic wheeze and no choking history (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign Body Aspiration).
rationales.C: No fever, no consolidation, no systemic illness; antibiotics and steroids do not dislodge a foreign body. Empirical antibiotics would be appropriate only with established post-obstructive pneumonia (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: CT is more sensitive than CXR but adds radiation without changing management; rigid bronchoscopy is both diagnostic and therapeutic. CT is appropriate only in atypical or chronic presentations where diagnosis is unclear (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign Body Aspiration).

rationales.A: Daily ICS is for the Modified Asthma Predictive Index pattern: frequent symptoms, interval symptoms, or over 2-3 severe attacks/year. Infrequent viral-triggered wheeze without interval symptoms does not meet criteria. Would be correct with mAPI-positive or frequent severe attacks (Source: National Asthma Handbook).
rationales.B: Montelukast has only modest benefit in preschool wheeze, is not preferred first-line maintenance, and carries an FDA black-box neuropsychiatric warning. Considered only when ICS not tolerated (Source: National Asthma Handbook).
rationales.D: Pre-emptive high-dose ICS at URTI onset has evidence only in severe preschool wheezers and risks growth velocity reduction. Would be appropriate only with severe recurrent attacks failing other strategies under specialist input (Source: National Asthma Handbook).
rationales.E: Specialist referral is for diagnostic uncertainty, severe/frequent attacks, failure of standard therapy, or atypical features; this is textbook preschool viral wheeze and is managed in primary care. Would be correct with poor response to standard step-up (Source: National Asthma Handbook).

rationales.A: Dexamethasone alone is for mild croup (Westley 0-2); stridor at rest and marked recession is moderate (Westley 3-7) and requires nebulised adrenaline plus 4-hour observation for rebound. Would be correct for mild croup with no stridor at rest (Source: RCH CPG: Croup).
rationales.C: Epiglottitis presents with drooling, tripod posture, toxic appearance and muffled (not hoarse) voice, typically in unimmunised children; this presentation is viral croup. Would be correct only with epiglottitis features (Source: RCH CPG: Croup).
rationales.D: Humidified air and heliox have no proven benefit; deferring steroid is harmful as steroid reduces admission and return visits. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Croup).
rationales.E: Salbutamol/ipratropium addresses bronchospasm not subglottic oedema; treating croup as asthma misses the pathology. Would be correct for acute asthma with audible wheeze and recession (Source: RCH CPG: Croup).

rationales.A: Montelukast is an alternative add-on but less effective than LABA at this step in children 6 plus; AU guidelines prefer ICS-LABA as primary step-up. Would be considered only if LABA contraindicated or not tolerated (Source: National Asthma Handbook).
rationales.C: MART (as-needed budesonide-formoterol) is the preferred adult/adolescent strategy but not the primary step-up for a 10-year-old already on regular ICS with poor control; combination ICS-LABA is preferred. Would be appropriate from age 12 with structured MART introduction (Source: National Asthma Handbook).
rationales.D: Biologics (omalizumab, mepolizumab) require failure of high-dose ICS-LABA with optimised adherence; jumping to biologic without intermediate step-up is the over-escalation error. Would be correct only after high-dose ICS-LABA failure (Source: National Asthma Handbook).
rationales.E: LAMA (tiotropium) is add-on for severe asthma uncontrolled on medium-to-high-dose ICS-LABA, not first step-up from low-dose ICS. Would be appropriate at later step after ICS-LABA failure (Source: National Asthma Handbook).

rationales.A: Amoxicillin does not cover Bordetella pertussis; macrolides are required. Apnoea in an under-6-month infant warrants admission, and pertussis is notifiable on clinical suspicion. Would be correct only for a different bacterial cause (Source: CDNA Pertussis SoNG; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Azithromycin and admission are correct but salbutamol does not relieve pertussis spasm, and contact tracing plus household post-exposure prophylaxis is mandatory under CDNA. Would be appropriate without the salbutamol and with the public health step (Source: CDNA Pertussis SoNG).
rationales.D: Erythromycin is associated with infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis in under-1-month infants and has more GI side effects than azithromycin; under-6-months with apnoea must be admitted. Would be acceptable only for older children when azithromycin unavailable (Source: CDNA Pertussis SoNG).
rationales.E: Ceftriaxone and prednisolone do not treat pertussis; notification on clinical suspicion is required to begin contact tracing immediately. Would never be correct (Source: CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

rationales.A: Yellow zone by definition requires intensification; 2 puffs 4-hourly equals background PRN and misses the early deterioration. Would be appropriate only in the green zone (Source: National Asthma Handbook Action Plan).
rationales.B: Home-initiated prednisolone is for selected children with frequent severe exacerbations under specialist tailored plans, not routine yellow-zone primary-care advice. Would be appropriate with a documented specialist plan (Source: National Asthma Handbook).
rationales.C: Stopping the preventer during deterioration worsens control by removing anti-inflammatory cover; preventer is continued or intensified. Would never be correct (Source: National Asthma Handbook).
rationales.E: Mandating ED for any salbutamol increase produces unnecessary attendances and undermines self-management; the plan needs graded thresholds (home/GP/ED). Would be appropriate only for red-zone criteria (Source: National Asthma Handbook).

rationales.B: Parental presence systematically suppresses disclosure of risk behaviour; HEEADSSS is conducted with the adolescent alone after explicit confidentiality framing. Would be reasonable only as warm-up small talk before the parent leaves (Source: RACP Adolescent Health GP Resource Kit).
rationales.C: HEEADSSS deliberately starts with low-threat domains (Home, Education) and saves Sexuality and Suicide for after rapport. Opening with high-threat domains is the disclosure-killing anti-pattern (Source: RACP GP Resource Kit).
rationales.D: Every adolescent encounter is a HEEADSSS opportunity; deferral risks missing the disclosure the guarded presentation suggests. Would be acceptable only if the patient refuses, with explicit return-to-it next visit (Source: RACP GP Resource Kit).
rationales.E: Self-administered screens can supplement but never replace structured face-to-face interview; subtle cues and follow-up questions are lost. Would be appropriate only as an adjunct to direct interview (Source: NSW Health Youth Health Better Practice Framework).

rationales.A: Unconditional promises misrepresent the legal duty to disclose risk of serious harm and destroy trust when a later break becomes necessary. Would never be correct; framing the limits up-front protects the relationship (Source: RACP GP Resource Kit).
rationales.C: Parental disclosure is not automatic under 16 in Australia; the Gillick framework permits confidential care if the adolescent is competent. Would be correct only if the adolescent is not Gillick-competent and parental involvement is in their best interest (Source: RACGP; RACP).
rationales.D: Confidentiality limits are tied to risk of serious harm and abuse, not to which topic is discussed; carving out drug use as private misrepresents the duty. Would never be correct (Source: RACP GP Resource Kit).
rationales.E: Confidential adolescent care without a parent present is the recommended model; parental consent is not required to interview a Gillick-competent young person. Would be appropriate only if the adolescent is clearly not Gillick-competent (Source: RACGP Adolescent Confidentiality Guidance).

rationales.A: AU law follows Gillick: a competent minor may consent to contraception without parental involvement. Blanket parental-consent rule is the legal anti-pattern. Would be appropriate only if Gillick competence is not established (Source: Family Planning NSW; RACGP).
rationales.B: NSW mandatory reporting is triggered by risk of significant harm, not consensual age-appropriate sex in a non-exploitative relationship. Would be correct if the partner were much older, coercion present, or other RoSH features apply (Source: NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide).
rationales.D: Withholding LARC from a competent minor who has requested it and is at ongoing pregnancy risk is not consistent with Gillick. Condoms-only delay would be appropriate only if she chose that method after counselling (Source: Family Planning NSW).
rationales.E: Parental involvement is encouraged but never a precondition once Gillick competence is documented. Would be correct only if competence not established and the adolescent agrees to involve a parent (Source: RACGP Adolescent Confidentiality Guidance).

rationales.A: TOP under the Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008 does not require court authorisation for competent minors; Gillick is the test. Would be correct only if no Australian jurisdiction had legislated the test (i.e., never) (Source: RANZCOG; Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008).
rationales.B: Pregnancy in an under-16 is not itself mandatorily reportable in Victoria; threshold is significant harm considering age difference, coercion and exploitation. Would be correct if the partner is significantly older or coercion present (Source: Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting).
rationales.C: Gillick applies to all medical decisions including TOP; parental consent is encouraged not required. Would be correct only with proven incompetence (Source: RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Mandated cooling-off is not part of Victorian termination law and delay risks pushing past medical-TOP window (under 9 weeks). Would never be correct as a statutory step (Source: RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Parental substance use alone does not meet the NSW RoSH threshold; the question is whether current circumstances place the child at risk. Would be correct if there were evidence of active child neglect or harm now (Source: NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide).
rationales.B: Documenting without using the Mandatory Reporter Guide decision tool risks missing the RoSH threshold and is not consistent with reporter duties. Would be appropriate only after MRG indicates below-threshold (Source: NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide).
rationales.C: Informal kinship care does not extinguish the reporter's duty; the MRG must still be applied. Would be correct only after MRG confirms below-threshold (Source: NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide).
rationales.D: Police are notified for criminal matters but child welfare goes via the Child Protection Helpline after using the MRG. Would be correct in parallel for active criminal violence, not as the sole pathway for welfare concerns (Source: NSW Health Child Wellbeing Unit).

rationales.A: Any bruise in a non-cruising infant is TEN-4-FACESp positive and a sentinel injury; 'normal variant' is the dismissal anti-pattern. Would be acceptable only in a mobile cruiser/walker with bruising in expected sites (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse).
rationales.B: Bleeding-disorder screen is appropriate but does not replace urgent safeguarding for a sentinel injury; running coags alone is the medicalisation-of-abuse anti-pattern. Would be sufficient only if safeguarding has already cleared and there is family bleeding history (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse).
rationales.C: Single-region X-ray misses clinically silent rib, metaphyseal and skull fractures; skeletal survey is the standard imaging for suspected NAI under 2 years. Would be appropriate only in older children with localised injury and no safeguarding concern (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse; RANZCR).
rationales.E: Photo-and-defer misses time-critical assessment for sentinel injury in a non-mobile infant. Documentation supplements but does not replace acute admission and notification (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse).

rationales.B: Repeat skeletal survey at 11-14 days substantially improves detection of healing rib and metaphyseal fractures; single survey alone is incomplete. Would be acceptable only in older mobile children with localised injury (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse; RANZCR).
rationales.C: MRI brain alone misses occult fractures; skeletal survey is mandatory even without external fracture evidence because clinically silent fractures are common. Would never alone be sufficient (Source: RANZCR).
rationales.D: Retinal haemorrhage detection requires dilated indirect ophthalmoscopy by an ophthalmologist; bedside undilated fundoscopy misses retinal haemorrhages. Would never be sufficient (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse).
rationales.E: Sentinel injuries in non-mobile infants predict escalating abuse; outpatient follow-up alone is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse).

rationales.A: Exposure is confirmed; this category does not apply. Would be correct only when prenatal exposure history cannot be confirmed but features are otherwise consistent (Source: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD).
rationales.B: ADHD may coexist but does not capture facial dysmorphology or the breadth of FASD-specific neurodevelopmental impairment. Would be correct as a stand-alone diagnosis only without FASD features (Source: DSM-5; Australian Guide to FASD).
rationales.C: He has all three sentinel features (smooth philtrum, thin upper vermilion, short palpebral fissures), so the less-than-three category does not apply (Source: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD).
rationales.D: FSIQ over 70 does not exclude FASD; diagnosis is made on neurodevelopmental domain impairment, not global IQ. Would be correct only for a non-FASD aetiology (Source: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD).

rationales.A: Multiple MARSIPAN-AU high-risk criteria (HR under 40, postural BP drop, hypothermia, hypokalaemia, hypoglycaemia, QTc over 450 ms) mandate inpatient medical admission; outpatient is the under-triage anti-pattern. Would be appropriate only with low-risk physical parameters (Source: ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU Guide).
rationales.C: Medical stabilisation precedes mental health admission in MARSIPAN-AU high-risk; aggressive 50 kcal/kg/day refeeding precipitates refeeding syndrome. Would be correct only after medical stabilisation and on a graded refeeding plan (Source: ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU).
rationales.D: Aggressive crystalloid bolus risks cardiac failure in the malnourished heart; cautious volume correction with electrolyte replacement is needed, not resuscitation-style fluids. Would be correct only in haemorrhagic or septic shock unrelated to malnutrition (Source: ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU).
rationales.E: Day program does not address immediate medical risk; high-risk physical criteria mandate inpatient admission. Would be correct only for medically stable patients with adequate community supports (Source: ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU).

rationales.A: MARSIPAN-AU medical admission criteria are not met (BMI normal, electrolytes near range, no bradycardia, normal QTc); inpatient admission is reserved for high physical risk. Would be correct only with high-risk vitals or electrolytes (Source: ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU).
rationales.B: Bulimia carries significant medical and psychological morbidity even at normal weight; reassurance-only misses the disorder. Would be correct only in occasional subthreshold disordered eating without functional impairment (Source: ANZAED Clinical Practice Standards).
rationales.C: Fluoxetine 60 mg may add benefit for bulimia in adults but CBT-ED is first-line; pharmacotherapy alone is not standard. Would be appropriate after CBT-ED failure or as add-on (Source: RANZCP Eating Disorder Guidelines).
rationales.E: Electrolyte replacement is supportive but does not treat the disorder; CBT-ED is the definitive first-line. Would be appropriate as a supportive adjunct alongside CBT-ED, not in isolation (Source: ANZAED Clinical Practice Standards).

rationales.A: Intranasal steroids help mild OSA with adenoidal hypertrophy, but witnessed apnoeas, hyperactivity and tonsillar hypertrophy require ENT and sleep referral first. Would be correct in mild snoring without apnoea or daytime impairment (Source: RCH CPG: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea).
rationales.B: Untreated OSA fully mimics ADHD; starting stimulants before addressing sleep is the classic misdiagnosis anti-pattern. Would be correct only after OSA is treated and ADHD persists (Source: RACP Australian Guideline on ADHD).
rationales.C: CPAP is reserved for residual OSA after adenotonsillectomy or where surgery is contraindicated; not first-line in a previously untreated child with surgical anatomy. Would be correct as second-line after failed surgery (Source: RCH CPG: OSA).
rationales.D: Snoring with witnessed apnoeas, behavioural change and morning headaches is clinical OSA, not a normal variant. Would never be correct in this presentation (Source: RCH CPG: OSA).

rationales.A: AADPA guidance requires systematic comorbidity assessment (anxiety, depression, learning disorder, sleep, substance use, trauma) before pharmacotherapy because comorbidities alter the plan. Would be appropriate after structured screen confirms uncomplicated ADHD (Source: AADPA ADHD Guideline).
rationales.B: Routine neuropsychological battery is not required to confirm ADHD or guide pharmacotherapy. Would be appropriate only for diagnostic uncertainty or suspected specific learning disorder (Source: AADPA ADHD Guideline).
rationales.D: School bullying management is part of the plan but does not justify deferring diagnostic completion. Would never be correct as the sole next step (Source: AADPA ADHD Guideline).
rationales.E: Sleep is one of multiple domains; isolated melatonin without comprehensive comorbidity assessment misses the broader picture. Would be correct only as adjunct after full screen (Source: AADPA ADHD Guideline).

rationales.A: Restricted interests, sensory features (toe walking, hand flapping), echolalia, monotone speech, and parallel play indicate ASD rather than ADHD alone. Would be correct only without social communication or restricted/repetitive features (Source: DSM-5; RACP).
rationales.C: Specific learning disorder does not explain social communication deficits and restricted/repetitive behaviours. Would be correct as a co-diagnosis but not the primary (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.D: Childhood-onset schizophrenia is rare and requires positive psychotic symptoms (delusions/hallucinations), absent here. Would be correct only with documented psychotic symptoms (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.E: Reactive attachment disorder requires a history of pathogenic care; not the picture in a stably parented child with restricted interests and stereotypies. Would be correct only with documented severe early caregiver disruption (Source: DSM-5).

rationales.A: Transition occurs at age 9, not 6, since the NDIS Early Childhood Approach was extended to under 9 in 2023. Would have been correct under the pre-2023 framework if it had set age 6 (Source: NDIS Early Childhood Approach).
rationales.B: Transition is age-driven (under 9), not purely family preference; families can engage early but the threshold is fixed. Would never be correct (Source: NDIS).
rationales.C: The original 7-year cut-off was raised in 2023 to under 9; the unchanged-since-launch claim is factually wrong. Would have been correct pre-2023 (Source: NDIS Early Childhood Approach).
rationales.D: No NDIS age threshold at 12; the Early Childhood Approach upper limit is under 9. Would never be correct (Source: NDIS).

rationales.A: She separates without distress at netball, weekends and overnight at grandmother's; distress is school-specific not separation-driven. Would be correct only with distress on all separations from primary attachment figures (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.B: She is distressed about not attending and not engaging in delinquent behaviour; this is the opposite of conduct disorder truancy. Would be correct only with rule violation and aggression (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.D: Pain occurs only on school mornings, with anticipatory distress and avoidance, and resolves at weekends; somatic symptoms are part of the anxiety pattern, not isolated functional pain. Would be correct only if pain were context-independent (Source: Raising Children Network School Refusal).
rationales.E: She engages happily on weekends and shows no anhedonia outside school; not depressive. Would be correct only with pervasive low mood and anhedonia across contexts (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders Guidelines).

rationales.B: Venlafaxine is not first-line in adolescents (discontinuation symptoms, less favourable safety); fluoxetine is preferred. Weekly suicidality monitoring is required regardless of agent. Would be correct only after SSRI failure under specialist care (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG).
rationales.C: Paroxetine is not recommended in adolescents due to short half-life, severe discontinuation and unfavourable risk-benefit. Would never be correct in this age group (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG).
rationales.D: TCAs are not effective in paediatric depression and carry overdose risk in an at-risk adolescent. Would never be correct for adolescent MDD (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG).
rationales.E: Combination psychological plus pharmacological therapy is recommended for moderate-severe adolescent depression; stopping therapy is the wrong direction. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG).

rationales.A: Gender-affirming hormones in under-16s are introduced through structured pathway with MDT consensus and informed consent including fertility; immediate testosterone without staged approach is not standard. Would be appropriate at a later stage after suppression and structured consent (Source: RCH Australian Standards of Care).
rationales.C: Court authorisation is no longer required for stage 1 puberty suppression in Australia after Re Jamie (2013) and Re Kelvin (2017) where parents and clinicians agree. Would never be required in current Australian practice (Source: RCH Australian Standards of Care).
rationales.D: Australian Standards explicitly do not require deferral to 18; staged medical intervention may begin earlier with MDT. Would be correct only with no MDT consensus or active contraindication (Source: RCH Australian Standards of Care).
rationales.E: Surgical intervention is not stage 1 in Australian adolescent gender-affirming care; reversible medical intervention precedes adult surgery. Would never be correct as the next step (Source: RCH Australian Standards of Care).

rationales.A: The Reportable Conduct Scheme covers schools, residential care and a wide range of organisations, not only classroom teachers. Would be correct only if the alleged perpetrator were outside an in-scope organisation (Source: Commission for Children and Young People Victoria).
rationales.B: Hospital admission does not remove the duty to notify Police of possible criminal conduct or to trigger the Reportable Conduct Scheme. Would never be correct (Source: Commission for Children and Young People Victoria).
rationales.D: Internal management does not satisfy mandatory reporting or the parallel Reportable Conduct Scheme obligations; both sit outside the school's HR process. Would never be correct (Source: Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting).
rationales.E: Reports are made on reasonable belief; forensic interview is arranged in parallel, not as a prerequisite. Delaying notification breaches the statutory duty. Would never be correct (Source: Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting).

rationales.A: Oral ciprofloxacin monotherapy is for early Pseudomonas eradication or mild outpatient exacerbations; FEV1 drop of 17 points with consolidation, weight loss and hypoxia mandates IV dual therapy and admission. Would be correct for mild exacerbation with preserved spirometry (Source: CF Foundation Pulmonary Guidelines).
rationales.B: Ceftriaxone does not reliably cover Pseudomonas; ceftazidime, piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime or meropenem is required. Azithromycin has anti-inflammatory but not adequate antipseudomonal effect in CF. Would be appropriate for non-CF CAP (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Flucloxacillin covers MSSA, which is supplementary not primary in chronic Pseudomonas exacerbation; rifampicin adds no value and is hepatotoxic. Would be appropriate with confirmed MSSA exacerbation and no Pseudomonas (Source: CF Foundation Pulmonary Guidelines).
rationales.D: Carbapenem monotherapy is reserved for multi-resistant Pseudomonas or beta-lactam allergy; standard is beta-lactam plus aminoglycoside to preserve carbapenems and exploit synergy. Would be appropriate with documented multi-resistant Pseudomonas (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines; CF Australia).

rationales.B: Cefalexin is broader than required and first-line oral when needed is flucloxacillin (or TMP-SMX in high-MRSA areas); oral therapy is not indicated for localised non-bullous impetigo. Would be correct only with widespread or systemic disease (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
rationales.C: Empirical MRSA cover is reserved for confirmed/strongly suspected MRSA (Top End/central Australia high-prevalence settings). Would be correct only in remote-community or known high-MRSA-prevalence settings (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: IV is for severe cellulitis, abscess, systemic toxicity, or oral failure, none present. Would be correct only with systemic toxicity or facial cellulitis (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Topical treatment shortens duration and limits spread; observation alone leaves school/household contacts exposed. Would be correct only for a single resolving lesion already crusted (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Measles requires fever PLUS cough/coryza/conjunctivitis with Koplik spots and face-down spread; none of those are present. Would be correct with the classic prodrome and Koplik spots (Source: NHMRC; Australian Immunisation Handbook).
rationales.C: Rubella features post-auricular/sub-occipital lymphadenopathy and face-down rash; neither is described. Would be correct with documented post-auricular nodes plus face-down rash (Source: CDNA Rubella SoNG).
rationales.D: Parvovirus B19 affects school-age children with slapped-cheek face and lacy limb rash, not a truncal post-fever rash in infants. Would be correct in a 5-10-year-old with facial slapped-cheek (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Scarlet fever requires GAS pharyngitis features (strawberry tongue, sandpaper rash, sore throat); absent here. Would be correct with prior GAS pharyngitis (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: This is the legacy blanket rule, superseded by NHMRC Staying Healthy 5th edition which does not require exclusion. Would have been correct under the older guideline (Source: NHMRC Staying Healthy 5th edition).
rationales.C: Primary HSV gingivostomatitis gives clustered anterior oral vesicles, gum inflammation and bleeding without acral palm/sole lesions. Would be correct only with gingival involvement and no acral lesions (Source: RCH CPG: HSV).
rationales.D: Varicella produces crops of vesicles on trunk/face/scalp evolving through stages, not acral vesicular sparing the trunk. Would be correct with classic centrifugal vesicular crops (Source: RCH CPG: Varicella).
rationales.E: EM has target lesions on limbs without palm-sole-buccal pattern; childcare exposure and classic distribution make HFMD overwhelmingly likely. Would be correct with target lesions and drug/infection trigger (Source: DermNet NZ).

rationales.A: Index case is no longer infectious after rash onset, but household contacts were exposed in the preceding viraemic week; brother with sickle cell disease and pregnant mother both need targeted action. Reassurance-only is the missed-aplastic-crisis trap (Source: RCH CPG: Parvovirus B19; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: First-line for maternal exposure is parvovirus IgG/IgM serology; if seroconverting, serial foetal ultrasound with MCA-PSV detects foetal anaemia and guides intrauterine transfusion. IVIG is not standard maternal therapy (Source: RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Foetal loss risk is under 10 percent overall (highest under 20 weeks); management is serology and surveillance, not termination. Would never be the first action (Source: RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Viraemia has resolved by the time the slapped-cheek rash appears; the child is no longer infectious and school exclusion is not required. Would never be correct after rash onset (Source: CDNA Parvovirus SoNG).

rationales.A: Documented wheeze with food trigger meets criteria for anaphylaxis, requiring the Anaphylaxis plan plus autoinjector; asthma comorbidity strengthens this. Would be correct for confirmed allergy without anaphylaxis features (Source: ASCIA Action Plans).
rationales.C: 300 microgram is prescribed from 20 kg upwards under ASCIA; at 17 kg the 150 microgram device is correct. Would be correct from 20 kg (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Action Plan).
rationales.D: Recent objective anaphylaxis with large SPT wheal and elevated specific IgE makes confirmatory challenge unsafe and unnecessary. Would be correct only for low-risk equivocal results without recent anaphylaxis (Source: ASCIA Oral Food Challenges).
rationales.E: Wheeze within 20 minutes of a known food trigger is anaphylaxis until proven otherwise; an asthma-only plan misses adrenaline prescription. Would never be correct in food-triggered wheeze (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Action Plan).

rationales.A: Sedating antihistamines are not recommended for paediatric eczema itch (no evidence of benefit, daytime sedation), and continuing insufficient steroid potency perpetuates flares. Would be correct only for short-term sleep disturbance in eczema flares as adjunct (Source: RCH CPG: Eczema; ASCIA).
rationales.C: Oral steroids cause rebound flares on withdrawal and are not recommended in paediatric atopic dermatitis except exceptional severe acute disease under specialist care. Would be correct only for severe acute flare under specialist oversight (Source: ASCIA; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Topical calcineurin inhibitors are useful steroid-sparing agents for flexures/face but are second-line in AU and not monotherapy in moderate disease. Would be correct as steroid-sparing addition on facial/flexural eczema (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Systemic immunomodulators are reserved for severe disease failing optimised topicals and phototherapy under paediatric dermatology; appropriate topical escalation has not been tried. Would be correct after failed maximised topical therapy (Source: RCH CPG: Eczema).

rationales.B: LEAP trial and ASCIA explicitly advise against delaying allergen introduction; delay increases not decreases food allergy risk. Would never be correct under current evidence (Source: ASCIA Infant Feeding and Allergy Prevention).
rationales.C: Pre-screening with SPT in asymptomatic infants is not recommended because it identifies sensitisation, not clinical allergy, and leads to unnecessary avoidance. Would be correct only in infants with prior reaction (Source: ASCIA Infant Feeding and Allergy Prevention).
rationales.D: Routine in-clinic introduction is not recommended and is impractical; home introduction per ASCIA is the standard. Would be correct only for infants with prior reaction or severe eczema with documented sensitisation (Source: ASCIA).
rationales.E: Hydrolysed formulas are not recommended for allergy prevention and there is no reason to interrupt breastfeeding. Would never be correct for prevention (Source: NHMRC Infant Feeding Guidelines; ASCIA).

rationales.B: Reproducible urticaria during challenge meets failed-challenge criteria; this child is still allergic and unrestricted milk is unsafe. Would be correct only with no objective reaction across all dose steps and observation (Source: ASCIA Oral Food Challenges).
rationales.C: Baked milk ladder is specialist-directed; home titration after a failed open challenge is unsafe. Would be correct only after a specialist passes the baked milk ladder challenge (Source: ASCIA Milk allergy).
rationales.D: The challenge failed; substituting milk would expose to ongoing risk. Would never be correct after objective reaction (Source: ASCIA Oral Food Challenges).
rationales.E: IM adrenaline is reserved for anaphylaxis; isolated cutaneous reactions without respiratory/CV/persistent-GI involvement are treated with antihistamine and observation. Would be correct only for anaphylactic reaction during challenge (Source: ASCIA; ANZCOR 9.2.7).

rationales.A: Adrenaline is reserved for anaphylaxis (respiratory/CV/persistent severe GI); urticaria with angioedema but no airway or BP compromise does not meet criteria. Would be correct with stridor, wheeze, persistent vomiting or hypotension (Source: ASCIA Acute Anaphylaxis).
rationales.C: Promethazine is contraindicated under 2 years and not first-line in acute urticaria; corticosteroids have no acute benefit. Would never be correct (Source: ASCIA: Urticaria; TGA Promethazine warning).
rationales.D: Continuing the NSAID and antibiotic that temporally preceded urticaria is unsafe pending allergy assessment; suspected triggers must be withheld. Would be correct only after specialist drug-allergy assessment clears them (Source: ASCIA: Drug allergy in children).
rationales.E: Omalizumab is specialist treatment for chronic spontaneous urticaria failing high-dose antihistamines, not acute ED therapy. Would be correct only in chronic refractory urticaria under specialist care (Source: ASCIA: Urticaria).

rationales.B: Irritant dermatitis from soap affects hands/wrists, not belt line and earlobes; fungal scraping is for tinea (annular with central clearing). Distribution is the giveaway anti-pattern (Source: DermNet NZ).
rationales.C: Atopic dermatitis involves antecubital and popliteal flexures, not contact-defined geometric patches; serum total IgE is not diagnostic. Would be correct with flexural pattern and atopic history (Source: RCH CPG: Eczema; ASCIA).
rationales.D: Tinea corporis is annular with raised scaly borders and central clearing; most dermatophytes do not fluoresce under Wood's lamp. Distribution and morphology are wrong here (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Discoid eczema is multiple coin-shaped lesions in random distribution; direct immunofluorescence is for immunobullous disease. Distribution wrong; technique wrong (Source: DermNet NZ).

rationales.A: Failure to treat all household and close contacts simultaneously is the commonest cause of treatment failure; topical steroids do not address infestation. Would never be correct as the index-only approach (Source: CARPA Standard Treatment Manual; RCH CPG: Scabies).
rationales.C: Ivermectin is an adjunct (crusted disease, outbreaks) and does not replace topical scabicide, contact treatment and environmental measures. Would be correct as add-on in crusted scabies (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Benzyl benzoate is alternative but irritant to paediatric skin and not first-line; 7 consecutive nights is not standard. Would be correct only as second-line when permethrin contraindicated (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Diagnosis is confirmed by microscopy; biopsy is unnecessary and delays definitive management of an outbreak. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Scabies).

rationales.B: Topical therapy alone is inadequate for tinea capitis because dermatophytes invade hair shafts beyond topical penetration; systemic therapy is mandatory. Would be correct only for tinea corporis without scalp involvement (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Kerion is an inflammatory dermatophyte reaction, not a bacterial abscess; I&D worsens scarring and antibiotics do not treat the fungus. Would be correct only for confirmed bacterial scalp abscess (Source: DermNet NZ: Kerion).
rationales.D: Steroid monotherapy without antifungal cover risks worsening infection; some specialists use steroids as adjunct WITH antifungal for severe kerion. Would be correct only as adjunct to antifungal under specialist input (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Delaying treatment risks permanent scarring alopecia and community transmission; empirical therapy based on clinical/microscopy is standard while awaiting culture. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Tinea).

rationales.B: Linear morphoea is sclerotic, indurated and causes atrophy or limb-length discrepancy; the soft, flat-topped papules here are classic lichen striatus. Would be appropriate only if the band were indurated or scleroatrophic (Source: DermNet NZ: Lichen striatus).
rationales.C: Moderately potent steroids carry skin-atrophy risk on a young child's limbs and are unnecessary for a benign self-limiting condition. Would be correct only for severe pruritus refractory to mild steroid (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Systemic immunosuppression has no role in self-limiting lichen striatus; this is the over-treatment anti-pattern. Would be correct only in linear morphoea with active inflammation (Source: DermNet NZ).
rationales.E: Candidiasis is intertriginous, moist and not arranged along Blaschko lines; morphology is wrong. Would be correct only with classic moist intertriginous beefy-red patches with satellite pustules (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: Notification in Australia is on clinical suspicion and cannot await lab confirmation; failing to isolate exposes susceptible pregnant contacts. Would never be correct (Source: CDNA Rubella SoNG).
rationales.C: MMR is contraindicated in pregnancy and post-symptom-onset vaccination does not modify current illness. Catch-up MMR is planned for the patient at convalescence. Would never be correct as the immediate action (Source: NCIRS Rubella; Australian Immunisation Handbook).
rationales.D: Meningococcal disease has non-blanching purpuric rash with systemic toxicity, not blanching face-down maculopapular rash with post-auricular nodes. Distribution and rash type wrong (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Measles requires Koplik spots, cough/coryza/conjunctivitis prodrome and a brick-red confluent face-down rash; absent here. Lymphadenopathy pattern fits rubella. Would be correct only with classic measles prodrome (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook).

rationales.A: Early discharge after severe anaphylaxis (asthma comorbid, repeat adrenaline) underestimates biphasic risk; this is the under-observation anti-pattern. Would be correct only for mild single-episode anaphylaxis resolving with one adrenaline dose (Source: ASCIA).
rationales.B: 4 hours is the minimum after uncomplicated anaphylaxis; severe features justify longer. Would be correct for uncomplicated mild reaction (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis).
rationales.D: He is currently stable without airway compromise; intubation/adrenaline infusion is for refractory anaphylaxis with airway or CV failure. Would be correct only with persistent hypotension or airway compromise (Source: ANZCOR 9.2.7; ASCIA).
rationales.E: Steroids do not reliably prevent biphasic reactions and 1 hour is inadequate observation. ASCIA prioritises observation duration over routine steroids. Would never be correct (Source: ASCIA).

rationales.A: Tryptase rises in mast-cell-mediated reactions, not bradykinin-driven HAE; adrenaline/steroids/antihistamines are ineffective in HAE attacks. Would be correct only in suspected anaphylaxis or mastocytosis (Source: ASCIA: Angioedema; Australasian HAE consensus).
rationales.C: IgE testing is irrelevant in HAE (bradykinin-mediated); antihistamines do not prevent attacks. Would never be correct (Source: ASCIA: HAE).
rationales.D: Bowel-wall oedema from HAE causes the colicky pain; isolated GI workup misses the unifying systemic diagnosis. Would be correct only with isolated GI symptoms and no angioedema (Source: ASCIA: HAE).
rationales.E: Long-term HAE prophylaxis decisions require confirmed diagnosis and specialist input; tranexamic acid is sometimes used in children but is not empirical first-line and does not address the acute attack. Would be correct only after confirmed diagnosis and specialist review (Source: ASCIA: HAE).

rationales.A: DRESS triad (drug exposure, eosinophilia, organ involvement) with facial oedema is diagnostic; continuing the trigger worsens organ injury. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Severe Cutaneous Adverse Reactions).
rationales.C: SJS/TEN is characterised by epidermal sloughing, Nikolsky sign and mucositis; none present here. Would be correct with mucositis and skin detachment (Source: DermNet NZ: SJS).
rationales.D: Kawasaki requires fever 5 days plus specific criteria and would not explain eosinophilia or a 5-week post-drug temporal link. Would be correct with fever 5 days plus 4-of-5 criteria (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki).
rationales.E: Aromatic anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine, phenobarbitone) cross-react in DRESS and SJS/TEN; switching within class risks recurrence. Would never be correct; switch to a non-aromatic agent (Source: ASCIA Drug Allergy in Children).

rationales.A: Long-term oral steroids cause adverse effects without sustained benefit and high relapse on withdrawal in chronic urticaria. Would be correct only as a short bridging course in severe flares (Source: ASCIA: Chronic Urticaria).
rationales.C: Sedating antihistamines have cognitive and developmental concerns in children and are not preferred add-on; up-titration of non-sedating is preferred. Would be correct as adjunct at bedtime for severe sleep disturbance (Source: ASCIA: Chronic Urticaria).
rationales.D: Adrenaline has no role in chronic urticaria; it is reserved for acute anaphylaxis. Would never be correct (Source: ASCIA).
rationales.E: Ciclosporin is fourth-line after both up-titrated antihistamine and omalizumab fail. Would be correct only at that step (Source: ASCIA: Chronic Urticaria).

rationales.A: Invasive bacterial soft-tissue infection complicating varicella with septic shock physiology - discharge risks death; this is the missed-nec-fasc anti-pattern. Would be correct only for simple post-varicella superinfection without shock (Source: RCH CPG: Necrotising Fasciitis).
rationales.C: Topical and oral therapy are inadequate for invasive soft-tissue infection with septic shock physiology. Would be correct only for localised cellulitis without systemic features (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: IVIG is an adjunct in streptococcal TSS and nec fasc but does not replace early empirical IV antibiotics and surgical source control. Would be correct as adjunct after antibiotics and source control commenced (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Nec fasc is a clinical diagnosis; imaging must not delay resuscitation, antibiotics or surgery. Would be correct only as a deferred adjunct if uncertain after surgical exploration (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: Continuing the culprit drug is unequivocally harmful in SJS/TEN; oral steroid monotherapy alone does not change mortality and chlorhexidine to denuded mucosa injures further. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: SCAR).
rationales.C: Staphylococcal scalded skin occurs predominantly in young infants, has minimal mucosal involvement and intact mucosae; this case has temporal drug link plus mucositis. Would be correct in an infant with intact mucosae and positive culture (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Kawasaki has polymorphous rash and mucosal change but not skin sloughing, Nikolsky sign or atypical targets, and lacks a culprit drug link. Would be correct with fever 5 days plus Kawasaki criteria (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki).
rationales.E: Biopsy confirms but must not delay culprit withdrawal, transfer or supportive care in a mucocutaneous emergency. Would be correct as adjunct after initial stabilisation (Source: DermNet NZ: SJS).

rationales.B: AU primary-care guidance accepts clinical diagnosis of Still's murmur when all innocent features are present; routine echo is over-investigation. Would be correct only with any red-flag feature (diastolic component, harsh quality, abnormal pulses, failure to thrive) (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: No Jones features and no sore throat history; investigating ARF in an asymptomatic child with classic innocent murmur is inappropriate. Would be correct only in high-risk populations with sore throat history and Jones features (Source: RHDAustralia).
rationales.D: HCM has a harsh systolic murmur that INCREASES with Valsalva (opposite of this), often family history of sudden death. Would be correct only with that profile (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Small VSD murmur is harsh, pansystolic, often with thrill, and does not soften with Valsalva; the described musical dynamic murmur is innocent. Would be correct only with the pansystolic VSD profile (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Adenosine treats SVT, not a hypercyanotic spell; rhythm is sinus tachycardia from right-to-left shunting. Would be correct in narrow-complex SVT not responding to vagal manoeuvres (Source: RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot).
rationales.C: Frusemide reduces preload and worsens right-to-left shunting in a tet spell; volume expansion not depletion is needed. Would never be correct in a tet spell (Source: RCH CPG: ToF).
rationales.D: PGE1 maintains ductal patency in duct-dependent neonatal lesions; at 8 months the duct is closed. Would be correct in a neonate with duct-dependent cyanotic lesion (Source: BMJ Best Practice: ToF).
rationales.E: Bicarbonate is reserved for documented severe acidosis after positioning, oxygen, fluids and morphine have failed. Would be correct only as final step in refractory tet spell with confirmed acidosis (Source: AHA scientific statement).

rationales.A: Spontaneous closure is common only for small muscular VSDs; failure to thrive and heart failure demand treatment. Would be correct only for a small muscular VSD without haemodynamic features (Source: RCH CPG: Heart Failure in Children).
rationales.B: Beta-blockers are not first-line in infant left-to-right shunt failure; diuretic plus afterload reduction is standard. Would be correct in tet-spell prophylaxis or other specific indications (Source: BMJ Best Practice: VSD).
rationales.D: PGE1 is for duct-dependent lesions; a VSD with left-to-right shunt does not depend on the duct. Would be correct in duct-dependent cyanotic lesions (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.E: Supplemental oxygen lowers PVR and worsens pulmonary overcirculation in a VSD; this is the textbook iatrogenic error in left-to-right shunts. Would be correct only for documented hypoxaemia from another cause (Source: RCH CPG: Heart Failure).

rationales.A: PGE1 keeps the duct open in duct-dependent lesions; here a haemodynamically significant PDA needs closure, not patency. Would be correct only for duct-dependent neonatal cyanotic lesions (Source: RCH NICU PDA Guideline).
rationales.B: Conservative is reasonable for non-haemodynamically significant PDA; this infant has clear significance (escalating ventilation, wide pulse pressure, reversed diastolic flow). Would be correct only for small non-significant PDAs (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.C: Surgical ligation is reserved for failure or contraindication of medical therapy; pharmacological closure is first-line. Would be correct after failed COX-inhibitor course or contraindication (Source: RCH NICU CPG: PDA).
rationales.E: Paracetamol is an emerging second-line as a 3-7 day course when COX inhibitors are contraindicated, not indefinite or first-line. Would be correct as a short course with contraindication to indomethacin/ibuprofen (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.A: Diuresis is adjunctive for pulmonary congestion but does not address lethal ductal closure in duct-dependent systemic circulation. Would be correct only as adjunct once PGE1 has restored ductal flow (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Cardiology).
rationales.B: Vitamin K does not treat duct closure; PGE1 is required to reopen the duct. Would be correct as routine post-birth, not as immediate management of duct-dependent shock (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Coarctation).
rationales.C: Supplemental O2 further constricts the ductus and accelerates collapse in duct-dependent systemic circulation; target SpO2 is 85-95 percent, not over 95. Would be correct only with confirmed non-cardiac hypoxaemia (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: No narrow-complex tachycardia described; differential pulses and saturation differential are diagnostic of coarctation. Would be correct in SVT (Source: RCH CPG: SVT).

rationales.B: Unsynchronised defibrillation is for VF/pulseless VT; unstable SVT needs synchronised cardioversion at 0.5-1 J/kg. Would be correct in pulseless VT or VF (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 12.6).
rationales.C: Verapamil is contraindicated under 12 months (risk of refractory hypotension and cardiac arrest). Would be correct only in older children with stable SVT and no Wolff-Parkinson-White pre-excitation (Source: AMH Children's Dosing Companion).
rationales.D: Oral beta-blockade is for chronic prevention, not acute termination in an unwell infant. Would be correct only as maintenance after acute conversion (Source: RCH CPG: SVT).
rationales.E: Carotid sinus massage is not used in infants; ice to the face is the recommended vagal manoeuvre at this age. Would be correct only in older children/adolescents and adults (Source: ANZCOR 12.6).

rationales.A: QTc over 480 ms with exertional syncope and family history meets LQTS diagnostic criteria; reassurance is the missed-LQTS anti-pattern. Would be correct only with borderline QTc and no syncope/family history (Source: Heart Rhythm Society/AHA expert consensus).
rationales.C: Sotalol prolongs QT and is contraindicated in LQTS; non-QT-prolonging beta-blockers (propranolol, nadolol) are preferred. Would never be correct in LQTS (Source: AMH; BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.D: ICD is reserved for arrest survivors or breakthrough syncope on beta-blocker; first-line is beta-blocker plus lifestyle modification. Would be correct after beta-blocker failure (Source: AHA/HRS consensus).
rationales.E: Exertional trigger, family sudden death, and prolonged QTc point to LQTS not epilepsy; hypoxic limb jerking mimics seizure but is not seizure. Would be correct only with confirmed seizure on EEG (Source: BMJ Best Practice: LQTS).

rationales.A: Fever is a minor criterion, not mandatory; diagnosis requires 2 major or 1 major + 2 minor with GAS evidence. Would be correct only if fever were a stated mandatory feature (it is not) (Source: RHDAustralia 2020 Jones).
rationales.B: Carditis is one of several majors, not mandatory; absence does not exclude ARF if other criteria met. Would never be correct as a rule-out (Source: RHDAustralia 2020).
rationales.D: In high-risk populations monoarthritis/polyarthralgia can also be a major; polyarthritis remains a major giving definite (not probable) ARF when criteria met. Would never be correct (Source: RHDAustralia 2020).
rationales.E: Anti-DNase B is accepted GAS evidence in addition to ASOT; the criteria are met. Would never be correct (Source: RHDAustralia 2020).

rationales.A: Persistent fever over 5 days with KD features in an infant under 6 months mandates urgent assessment for coronary artery disease, not discharge. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki).
rationales.B: Persistently negative cultures and no antibiotic response shift the differential to Kawasaki; continued empirical antibiotics delay IVIG. Would be correct only with culture-positive infection (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Kawasaki).
rationales.C: Echo is essential and arranged urgently, but treatment with IVIG must not be delayed pending echo in incomplete KD with supportive labs. Would be correct only if echo could be done immediately at no delay to IVIG (Source: AHA Kawasaki).
rationales.E: Steroids are an adjunct (high-risk patients, IVIG-resistant disease), not monotherapy; IVIG remains first-line. Would be correct only as adjunct alongside IVIG in high-risk patients (Source: AHA Kawasaki).

rationales.A: Transient abnormal myelopoiesis usually self-resolves and does not require LP at presentation; cardiac and surgical assessment take priority. Would be correct only with persistent blast cells and CNS signs (Source: RCH CPG: Down Syndrome).
rationales.B: Routine MRI brain is not part of newborn Down syndrome assessment. Would be correct only with focal neuro signs or seizures (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Hip US is performed at 6 weeks (or earlier with clinical signs); not a 24-hour priority over cardiac/surgical assessment. Would be correct at scheduled 6-week time-point (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Congenital hypothyroidism is screened on the newborn bloodspot at 48-72 hours; important but lower priority than urgent cardiac assessment with duodenal atresia. Would be correct as planned screen, not the highest priority now (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Lymphoedema is due to congenital lymphatic dysplasia and does not require renal artery imaging; cardiac assessment is the priority. Would never be correct (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Turner Syndrome).
rationales.C: Atlanto-axial instability is associated with Down syndrome, not Turner; webbed neck reflects cystic hygroma resolution. Would be correct in Down syndrome with neck signs (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Widely spaced nipples are a dysmorphic feature without imaging indication in the newborn period. Would never be correct (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.E: Thyroid disease in Turner is monitored over time via TFTs, not via newborn US based on hairline. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Turner Syndrome).

rationales.A: Noonan features pulmonary stenosis (not supravalvular AS), hypertelorism with downslanting fissures and webbed neck, without hypercalcaemia. Would be correct with pulmonary stenosis plus Noonan facies (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Noonan).
rationales.C: 22q11.2 typically presents with conotruncal defects (truncus, IAA type B, ToF), hypocalcaemia, and immunodeficiency. Would be correct with conotruncal lesion and hypocalcaemia (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
